summary:
The State of Jin (c. 1033–376 BC) was one of the most powerful Zhou feudal states, located in the middle Yellow River valley (modern Shanxi province), that dominated northern China during the Spring and Autumn period as the leading hegemon. Under Duke Wen of Jin (r. 636–628 BC), Jin achieved the coveted title of hegemon (ba) after the decisive battle of Chengpu (632 BC) against Chu — the first great battle of competing Zhou states for dominance over the interstate system.

Jin's political history illustrates a recurring pattern in Chinese history: the gradual erosion of the ruling house's authority by powerful ministerial families. The Jin court was dominated by six powerful noble clans who competed for control, gradually reducing the Jin ruler to a figurehead. The state was eventually partitioned by the three dominant families — Han, Wei, and Zhao — in 453 BC, a partition formally recognized by the Zhou king in 403 BC. This 'Partition of Jin' (Sanjia fen Jin) is traditionally taken as the starting point of the Warring States period, and the three successor states of Han, Wei, and Zhao became three of the Seven Warring States. Jin's experience became a cautionary tale about the dangers of ministerial power versus royal authority.
